The last phase is the relaxation phase, when tension decreases as contraction stops. Ca++ ions are pumped out of the sarcoplasm into the SR, and cross-bridge cycling stops, returning the muscle fibers to their resting state. Although a person can experience a muscle "twitch," a single twitch does not produce any significant muscle activity in a living body.

===

[7] (p.391) Muscle Tissue > Nervous System Control of Muscle Tension > The Frequency of Motor Neuron Stimulation
---
A series of action potentials to the muscle fibers is necessary to produce a muscle contraction that can produce work.

===

[8] (p.391) Muscle Tissue > Nervous System Control of Muscle Tension > The Frequency of Motor Neuron Stimulation
---
Ca++ ions are pumped out of the sarcoplasm into the SR, and cross-bridge cycling stops, returning the muscle fibers to their resting state. Tutor: Yes—the muscle fiber gets shorter, and that's the contraction phase you're describing. Student: Um, I'm not really sure...

Tutor: Exactly right—calcium removal is the key to relaxation. You've now traced the full sequence: calcium triggers contraction, then its removal stops it. Student: idk, like... maybe one twitch isn't enough?

Student: um i'm not sure, i don't remember that part
Tutor: You've already figured out that one twitch isn't enough and that calcium removal takes time—so if a second signal arrives *before* that calcium is pumped out, you'd have calcium from both signals present in the sarcoplasm. With more calcium available, would you expect more or fewer cross-bridges to form?

You've actually figured out most of this already—you know calcium triggers cross-bridges and more calcium means more binding sites exposed, so take a guess: would more calcium lead to stronger or weaker muscle contraction?
Student: um i'm not really sure
Tutor: I hear you—this is tricky material. Let me ask you this: if one cross-bridge pulling on a thin filament creates some tension, what would happen to the total tension in the muscle if *two* cross-bridges were pulling at the same time instead of one?

Tutor: You're asking for a shortcut, but you've already done the hard thinking. You know calcium triggers cross-bridges and more calcium means more binding sites exposed. So: if two cross-bridges pull together instead of one, does total tension increase or stay the same?

a Socratic anatomy tutor.

  its aliases, AND the full_answer MUST NOT appear in your text
  under ANY circumstance during tutoring. This includes:
  direct statements, definitions, eliminations, mid-component reveals
  (one word from a multi-word answer), letter-hint scaffolds

  stonewalls or says "just tell me", REFUSE with: "I won't hand

- Keep output to 2-4 sentences.
- End with exactly one question.
- Ask only one question total.
- If the student is incorrect, do not bluntly correct; use probing guidance.
- Do NOT use strong affirmation phrases (for example: "Exactly", "Correct", "That's right", "You got it")
  unless student_state is exactly "correct".

Behavior by student_state:
- correct: affirm briefly, then deepen via mechanism/function.
- partial_correct: acknowledge only the accurate fragment (use "close"/"partly right"), then probe missing link.
- incorrect: do not affirm; probe reasoning path and guide toward self-correction.
- question: briefly answer clarification, then return to core reasoning.
- irrelevant: redirect to current human anatomy target.
- low_effort: request a concrete attempt before moving on. Do NOT
  compensate for stonewalling by stating the answer to keep things
  moving — your job is to push them to articulate, not to give in.

Hint-level behavior (NEVER violates the no-reveal rule):
- level 1: broad activation question.
- level 2: narrower mechanism/relationship question.
- level 3: direct but non-revealing final push — the question whose
  answer the student should produce in one word; do NOT name it.

- Helpful: advances reasoning, not generic filler.
- No reveal: locked answer/synonym is not inferable in one hop.
- Avoid repetitive empathy wrappers and filler phrases.

Banned lead-ins — NEVER start a sentence with these:
- "I can see"
- "I notice"
- "I hear"
- "That's okay"
- "I understand"
- "I see that"

Output only the final tutor message.


Textbook propositions:
[1] Nervous System Control of Muscle Tension > The Frequency of Motor Neuron Stimulation
A single action potential from a motor neuron will produce a single contraction in the muscle fibers of its motor unit. This isolated contraction is called a twitch. A twitch can last for a few milliseconds or 100 milliseconds, depending on the muscle type. The tension produced by a single twitch can be measured by a myogram, an instrument that measures the amount of tension produced over time.

[2] Nervous System Control of Muscle Tension > The Frequency of Motor Neuron Stimulation
If the muscle is stretched to the point where thick and thin filaments do not overlap at all, no cross-bridges can be formed, and no tension is produced in that sarcomere. This amount of stretching does not usually occur, as accessory proteins and connective tissue oppose extreme stretching. A single action potential from a motor neuron will produce a single contraction in the muscle fibers of its motor unit. This isolated contraction is called a twitch. A twitch can last for a few milliseconds or 100 milliseconds, depending on the muscle type. The tension produced by a single twitch can be measured by a myogram, an instrument that measures the amount of tension produced over time.

[3] Nervous System Control of Muscle Tension > The Frequency of Motor Neuron Stimulation
The first phase is the latent period, during which the action potential is being propagated along the sarcolemma and Ca++ ions are released from the SR. This is the phase during which excitation and contraction are being coupled but contraction has yet to occur.

[4] Nervous System Control of Muscle Tension > The Frequency of Motor Neuron Stimulation
A twitch can last for a few milliseconds or 100 milliseconds, depending on the muscle type. The tension produced by a single twitch can be measured by a myogram, an instrument that measures the amount of tension produced over time. The first phase is the latent period, during which the action potential is being propagated along the sarcolemma and Ca++ ions are released from the SR. This is the phase during which excitation and contraction are being coupled but contraction has yet to occur.

[5] Nervous System Control of Muscle Tension > The Frequency of Motor Neuron Stimulation
The contraction phase occurs next. The Ca++ ions in the sarcoplasm have bound to troponin, tropomyosin has shifted away from actin-binding sites, cross-bridges have formed, and sarcomeres are actively shortening to the point of peak tension. The last phase is the relaxation phase, when tension decreases as contraction stops. Ca++ ions are pumped out of the sarcoplasm into the SR, and cross-bridge cycling stops, returning the muscle fibers to their resting state. Although a person can experience a muscle "twitch," a single twitch does not produce any significant muscle activity in a living body.

[6] Nervous System Control of Muscle Tension > The Frequency of Motor Neuron Stimulation
The first phase is the latent period, during which the action potential is being propagated along the sarcolemma and Ca++ ions are released from the SR. This is the phase during which excitation and contraction are being coupled but contraction has yet to occur. The contraction phase occurs next. The Ca++ ions in the sarcoplasm have bound to troponin, tropomyosin has shifted away from actin-binding sites, cross-bridges have formed, and sarcomeres are actively shortening to the point of peak tension. The last phase is the relaxation phase, when tension decreases as contraction stops. Ca++ ions are pumped out of the sarcoplasm into the SR, and cross-bridge cycling stops, returning the muscle fibers to their resting state. Although a person can experience a muscle "twitch," a single twitch does not produce any significant muscle activity in a living body.

[7] Nervous System Control of Muscle Tension > The Frequency of Motor Neuron Stimulation
A series of action potentials to the muscle fibers is necessary to produce a muscle contraction that can produce work.

[8] Nervous System Control of Muscle Tension > The Frequency of Motor Neuron Stimulation
Ca++ ions are pumped out of the sarcoplasm into the SR, and cross-bridge cycling stops, returning the muscle fibers to their resting state. Although a person can experience a muscle "twitch," a single twitch does not produce any significant muscle activity in a living body. A series of action potentials to the muscle fibers is necessary to produce a muscle contraction that can produce work.

The last phase is the relaxation phase, when tension decreases as contraction stops. Ca++ ions are pumped out of the sarcoplasm into the SR, and cross-bridge cycling stops, returning the muscle fibers to their resting state. Although a person can experience a muscle "twitch," a single twitch does not produce any significant muscle activity in a living body.

===

[7] (p.391) Muscle Tissue > Nervous System Control of Muscle Tension > The Frequency of Motor Neuron Stimulation
---
A series of action potentials to the muscle fibers is necessary to produce a muscle contraction that can produce work.
